summary: McKenzie reflects on a mistake he made by hosting his blog on a separate domain from his business site, Bingo Card Creator. Despite consistently advising others to put their blog on their main business domain to share PageRank, he failed to follow his own advice. The result: his blog achieved a higher PageRank (PR5) than his product site (PR4), meaning potential customers find his blog first and must click through to the product, losing traffic along the way.
key_points: Hosting a business blog on the same domain as your product shares PageRank and consolidates SEO authority
McKenzie's blog earned PR5 organically through valuable content while his product site only reached PR4 despite deliberate link-building efforts
The gap between PR4 and PR5 represents significant search traffic differences, especially in low-competition niches
Valuable content naturally attracts links from other blogs and aggregators, often outperforming deliberate SEO tactics
money_quotes: What can I say, I really wish that circa June, 2007 I had known enough about my own advice to take it.
The difference between PR4 and PR5 is a whole lot of searches, particularly for low-competition niches.
I now get an absolutely insane number of people who search for my very favorite search terms and come here first, then follow the link to my actual product site.
This is utterly insane not because of the magnitude, but because I know I must be losing a huge percentage of the potential traffic there.
Don't let it happen to you!
key_insight: Host your business blog on your main product domain so that the SEO authority your content earns directly benefits your product pages rather than forcing visitors through an unnecessary redirect.
